core.intro: Wherever you grow longan in Australia the essentials are the same: a frost-free spot, deep free-draining acid soil, a named air-layered or grafted tree, and a cool, dry winter to switch the tree from leaf to flower. Longan is the close cousin of the lychee and grows much the same way, but it stores far better and a mature tree takes a little more cold. The state sections cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere you can grow the tree.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Australian longans are sold as named cultivars, and the choice matters because they crop very differently here. Kohala, a Hawaiian selection, is the popular all-rounder: early, vigorous, sweet and mild, and the least fussy about a cool winter, so it is the one most likely to set fruit in a marginal year. The best-flavoured types are the late Thai selections, and they are widely sold: Haew is a crisp, sweet, high-quality table longan, Chompoo carries a faint pink tinge in crisp well-flavoured flesh, and Biew Kiew is a heavy, comparatively regular cropper with large crisp cream-coloured flesh. Steer clear of unnamed seedlings, since most of the longan first grown in Australia was thin-fleshed and poor, and treat Daw, the Thai favourite, with caution because it sets badly in Australian conditions year after year. Longan shares its needs and many of its pests with its close relative the lychee.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: A single longan will set a crop on its own. The tree carries both male and female flowers together on a large terminal panicle and is pollinated by insects, with the honey bee the main carrier, so what really lifts your crop is strong bee activity and warm, dry, still weather across the few weeks of flowering, not a second variety planted nearby. The catch is the cultivar rather than the partner: a few types set poorly here whatever you do, most notably Daw, so the surer path to fruit is a dependable variety served by plenty of bees.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Plant in full sun in deep, free-draining, slightly acidic soil (about pH 5.5 to 6.5) in a frost-free position with good shelter from wind. Longan grows fast and its wood is brittle, so young trees blow over or shed limbs easily: stake them firmly, and in cyclone country keep them low and consider mounding soil around the butt as Asian growers do. The tree hates wet feet and salty ground, and a young longan can die after only five to ten days in waterlogged soil, while on high-pH soils it shows nutrient deficiencies that need correcting. Mulch heavily to keep the shallow feeder roots cool and moist. Warm soil gets a new tree away fastest, so spring into early summer is the usual planting time (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Longan is shallow-rooted, so keep the soil evenly moist under a thick mulch through the warm growing season, and above all while the fruit is filling, when going short cuts both size and yield. The exception is late summer through winter: easing right back on water then helps stop soft leafy growth and tips the tree toward flowering, the same job the cool dry season does. Feed along the lines used for lychees, leaning on potassium and going easy on nitrogen, since too much nitrogen drives leaves instead of flowers, and the trees run short of trace elements on poorer ground and repay a leaf or soil test. Drainage matters as much as watering, because the roots rot in soil that stays wet.

sections.heading: Irregular and biennial bearing
sections.body: The hardest thing about longan is that it does not crop evenly. Even the better varieties tend to bear heavily one year and lightly or not at all the next, and Australian trials saw as few as three good crops in five or six seasons. Part of the cause is the cool dry winter the tree needs to flower, because a mild or wet winter simply skips a crop; part is a tree exhausting itself after a bumper year; and part is heavy post-harvest pruning that strips too much wood and leaves too little time for the new growth that carries next year's flowers. Prune lightly and promptly after picking, thin a very heavy set, and choose a comparatively regular variety such as Biew Kiew if steady cropping matters to you.

sections.heading: Harvest, storing and drying
sections.body: Longan ripens in hanging clusters through late summer into autumn, broadly January to April in most districts and later where the season is cool. The fruit does not sweeten after picking, so harvest only once the brown skin is full and a sample tastes sweet (the flesh runs to roughly 18 to 21 percent sugar). The great advantage over lychee is keeping quality: the fruit holds for several weeks in the fridge at about 5 to 10 degrees, the skin darkening while the flesh stays good, and it freezes, cans and dries cleanly, with dried longan (the dragon's eye) a staple of Chinese cooking. A grafted or air-layered named tree usually starts bearing about three to five years after planting.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, air-layered (marcot) or grafted tree, never an unnamed seedling, because longan does not come true from seed and a seedling gives unpredictable fruit. If you buy a grafted tree, look for a clean union and dark, glossy leaves, since longan is prone to graft incompatibility that leaves a stunted, dull-leaved tree; using a matched same-variety rootstock avoids it, which is one reason marcots are so popular. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties on sale right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How long until a longan tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted or air-layered (marcot) named variety usually starts bearing about three to five years after planting, with useful crops a little later. A seedling can take six to eight years, may never crop well, and will not come true to its parent, which is why propagated named stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Can I grow a longan tree in a pot?
faqs.a: While it is young, yes. A longan will live in a large pot for a few years, which lets you nurse it through cold winters or stand it in your sunniest corner, but it is a big, long-lived tree that crops best planted out in a warm spot. Treat a pot as a way to start, or to keep a tree alive in a cool district, rather than a permanent home for a bearing tree.

faqs.q: How big does a longan tree get, and can I keep it smaller?
faqs.a: Left alone a longan can reach 9 to 12 metres, but it takes pruning well and most backyard growers top it after harvest to hold it at a pickable 3 to 4 metres. Keeping it low also makes it far easier to throw a net over the canopy as the fruit ripens.

faqs.q: How is a longan different from a lychee?
faqs.a: They are close cousins grown much the same way, but a longan is brown-skinned and musky-sweet where a lychee is red and perfumed, with a bigger seed and slightly less juicy flesh. A longan also stores and dries far better than a fragile lychee, a mature tree shrugs off a little more cold, and it often wants a touch more winter chill to flower well.

QLD.intro: Growing longan in Queensland. Queensland grows almost all of Australia's longan, from the Atherton Tableland and the tropical coast behind Cairns down through the central and southern fruit districts, so this is the state the crop was selected for.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland has the year-round warmth a longan wants, so the deciding factor is winter. The tree needs a cool, dry spell, ideally a couple of months with days under about 20 degrees, to flower well, and a mild or wet winter pushes it into leaf instead of flower. The drier, slightly cooler inland and tableland sites trigger flowering more reliably than the warm, humid coast, where a dependable annual crop is the hardest part of the job.

sections.heading: Where longan grows in QLD
sections.body: The crop took hold on the Atherton Tableland west of Cairns, around Mareeba and Walkamin, where the government trials that picked Australia's varieties were run. Plantings then run down the coast through the central and southern districts, including Townsville, Bundaberg and the Sunshine Coast hinterland, so your district, not just the state, sets which varieties and harvest dates suit you.

sections.heading: Which varieties suit QLD
sections.body: The full range crops somewhere in Queensland. In the warm north the early Hawaiian Kohala comes in first and is the safest bet where winters are mildest, while the late Thai types (Biew Kiew, Haew and Chompoo) reward the slightly cooler tableland and southern districts with their best flavour and size.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit ripens across summer into autumn. Early Kohala can start about late January, the main crop runs through February and March, and the late Thai selections hang on into March and April, a few weeks later again on the high tableland than down on the coast.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: The big problems are animals and a moth. Flying foxes, fruit bats and parrots strip ripening clusters, so netting is the standard defence. Macadamia nutborer is the major fruit pest, its larvae boring in toward the seed, and its damage is often blamed on fruit fly, which troubles longan far less. The fruit-piercing moth punctures ripening fruit at night, and in the warm north elephant beetle and scale (with sooty mould) turn up as well.

faqs.q: Do longan trees crop every year in Queensland?
faqs.a: Often not. Longan is naturally an on-and-off bearer, and even good Queensland orchards see a light year following a heavy one, made worse if a winter runs too warm or wet to set flowers. A dependable variety, a light prune straight after picking, and not letting the tree over-crop all help to even it out.

faqs.q: Can I grow longan on the humid Queensland coast?
faqs.a: Yes, the tree thrives, but expect lighter and less regular crops near the coast, since coastal winters often fail to deliver the trigger it needs. Choose an early, low-chill variety such as Kohala, give it your hottest and most open position, and treat a bumper season as a bonus.

WA.intro: Growing longan in Western Australia. Longan is a tropical tree, so in WA it belongs in the warm north: the Ord River around Kununurra and the irrigated Gascoyne around Carnarvon. It takes a touch more cold than its cousin the lychee, so a sheltered, frost-free Perth garden is worth a try, but flowering there is a gamble.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: A longan needs two things that WA can struggle to provide together: freedom from frost, and a cool, dry winter to set flowers (days under about 20 degrees for some weeks). The tropical north supplies the warmth and a distinct dry season; the Gascoyne is workable; and although a mature longan handles more cold than a lychee, the Swan Coastal Plain around Perth has mild, changeable winters that flower the tree only in some years. Treat a Perth longan as a long-term gamble rather than a sure crop.

sections.heading: Where longan grows in WA
sections.body: The Ord River Irrigation Area around Kununurra, in the East Kimberley, is WA's main tropical-fruit district and the best bet for longan. The irrigated Gascoyne around Carnarvon already grows a range of tropical and subtropical fruit and is the next option down. Around Perth and the South West the tree grows readily but fruits only in warm pockets and good years.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: In the tropical north the fruit ripens through the warm season from around January, a little earlier than the cooler eastern tablelands. Perth trees, in the years they do set, also ripen over summer but crop too unevenly to count on a date.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's distance and strict plant quarantine keep out several pests established in the eastern states, which is a genuine advantage, but birds and, in the north, flying foxes and fruit bats will still strip a ripening tree, so net backyard longans as the fruit colours up.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually certified and treated before entry, so many eastern nurseries will not post longan trees here. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: Can you grow a longan tree in Perth?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree, and because a mature longan is a little hardier than a lychee it comes through Perth winters, but fruiting is the catch: the mild, changeable cold season often fails to set flowers, so expect leafy growth and only the occasional crop. WA's dependable longan country is the tropical north.

faqs.q: Which longan variety suits WA's tropical north?
faqs.a: The early Hawaiian variety Kohala is the usual first choice for the warm north, being vigorous, sweet and the least demanding about winter chill. Start with clean, named, air-layered stock, and net the canopy as the fruit colours up.

NSW.intro: Growing longan in New South Wales. Longan is a frost-free, warm-climate crop in NSW, confined to the subtropical far north coast, where it ripens later than in Queensland. It edges a little further south than the lychee because a mature tree takes slightly more cold.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Longan needs warmth and a frost-free site, so in NSW it suits the humid, subtropical far north coast. The cold inland and the tablelands are out, because frost kills young trees, yet the tree still needs a cool (though not freezing) winter to flower. A mature longan tolerates a touch more cold than a lychee, which lets it creep a little further south, but a frost-free position remains the first requirement.

sections.heading: Where longan grows in NSW
sections.body: Longan is a minor crop here, grown in the same warm Northern Rivers country as lychee, around Lismore, Alstonville and Ballina and down toward Coffs Harbour. South of there the climate turns too cool and frost-prone for a dependable tree.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Northern NSW picks after Queensland. Its cooler, more southern position slows ripening, so the fruit tends to come in through late summer and autumn, with the late Thai varieties running latest of all.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Northern NSW shares Queensland's longan pests. Macadamia nutborer bores into the fruit (its damage is often mistaken for fruit fly, which bothers longan less), the fruit-piercing moth feeds on ripening fruit at night, and birds and flying foxes take the crop, so netting is the usual defence.

faqs.q: Is a longan more cold-hardy than a lychee in northern NSW?
faqs.a: A little. A mature longan has come through brief cold down toward minus 4 degrees, a notch past a lychee, so it ranges slightly further south. Young trees of both are tender, though, and a hard frost will still kill a new longan, so give it the warmest, most sheltered corner through its first few winters.

faqs.q: Can you grow a longan south of the far north coast?
faqs.a: It gets hard fast. Around Sydney and the cooler coast there is rarely enough summer warmth for good fruit and more frost risk for young trees, so try one only in the warmest, most sheltered pocket, or in a large pot you move under cover for winter, and count any fruit as a bonus.

VIC.intro: Growing longan in Victoria. Victoria is really too cold for longan: the tree is frost-tender when young and the winters do not suit reliable fruiting, so here a longan is a container or greenhouse curiosity rather than a backyard crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Longan wants a frost-free climate and a long warm season, and young trees are easily killed by frost. Victoria's cold, frosty winters sit outside what a young tree can take, so an outdoor longan in most of the state struggles to survive, let alone fruit. No Victorian district offers a dependable backyard crop, and commercial growing does not extend this far south.

sections.heading: Growing one under cover
sections.body: If you want to try anyway, grow your longan in a large pot you can move into a warm, bright, frost-free spot (a greenhouse, a sunroom, or hard against a sunny north-facing wall) over the cold months. Give it the warmest microclimate you have, shield it from frost every winter, and treat any fruit as a bonus. For a reliable crop in a cool climate, a hardier subtropical such as feijoa or loquat is a safer bet.

faqs.q: Can you grow a longan tree in Victoria?
faqs.a: Only with effort, and seldom with fruit. Victoria's frosts and cool summers do not suit longan, a frost-tender tropical tree. Your best chance is a potted tree kept frost-free and warm over winter, grown as a foliage plant that may occasionally surprise you with a crop.

faqs.q: Will a longan survive a Melbourne winter outdoors?
faqs.a: A young tree will probably be damaged or killed by frost, and even an older one rarely gets the heat it needs to crop. Grow it in a pot you can shift under cover for the cold months, or plant a hardier subtropical fruit instead.
